reasoning: Based on comprehensive evidence from sources dated before May 14, 2025, the probability that the Fed cuts rates before July 1 is low (13%). Key evidence: (1) Only ONE FOMC meeting remains (June 17-18) before the deadline (search_0_summary), (2) Fed held rates steady in May and explicitly cited uncertainty about trade shocks, taking a wait-and-see stance rather than signaling imminent cuts (search_1_summary), (3) Economic data as of May 7 showed unemployment stable at 4.2%, moderate payroll gains (+177K), and inflation described as 'somewhat elevated' – not indicating sharp deterioration (search_3_summary), (4) Market pricing showed <30% probability of a June cut, with expectations shifting toward July/September (search_0_summary, search_3_summary), (5) Fed officials reportedly 'less ready to discuss near-term cuts' as of mid-May (search_0_summary). The Fed typically requires clear, sustained economic weakness before cutting during pause periods, and there is insufficient time between May 14 and June 18 for data to deteriorate enough to justify a cut, barring an extreme shock.
